explanation: 'Ecco il mio libro' means 'Here is my book.' A possessive adjective in Italian needs the definite article in front of it: il mio, not bare mio. Both the article and mio agree with libro, which is masculine singular.

'Questa è la mia casa' means 'This is my house.' Casa is feminine singular, so both the definite article and the possessive adjective take the feminine form: la mia. Dropping the article, mia casa, is the classic slip.

'Questi sono i miei amici' means 'These are my friends.' The masculine plural of mio is the irregular-looking miei, and it keeps its definite article: i miei. The article is i, agreeing in the masculine plural.
'Dove sono le mie chiavi?' means 'Where are my keys?' Chiavi is feminine plural, so mio becomes mie with the article le: le mie. Watch the one-letter gap between miei (masculine) and mie (feminine).
'È questo il tuo telefono?' means 'Is this your phone?' For informal your the possessive adjective is tuo; it agrees with telefono, masculine singular, and takes the definite article: il tuo.
'Mi presti la tua penna?' means 'Will you lend me your pen?' Penna is feminine singular, so tuo agrees with the thing owned and becomes tua, with the article la: la tua.

'Anna cerca il suo cane' means 'Anna is looking for her dog.' Here is the trap: suo agrees with the thing owned, not the owner. Cane is masculine, so il suo, even though the owner Anna is female. English her tempts sua, but Italian looks at the dog.
examiner_note: Owner-agreement trap: her -> il suo because cane is m.sg. must_not_include catches la sua (agreeing with the owner).

explanation: 'Marco ama la sua macchina' means 'Marco loves his car.' Macchina is feminine, so the possessive is sua with the article la, la sua, even though the owner Marco is male. Suo/sua tracks the object owned, never the owner's gender.
examiner_note: Mirror of the her->il suo trap: his -> la sua because macchina is f.sg.

explanation: 'Questo è il nostro giardino' means 'This is our garden.' The our-form nostro is fully regular: it agrees with giardino, masculine singular, and takes the article, giving il nostro.
'La nostra scuola è grande' means 'Our school is big.' Nostro agrees with scuola, feminine singular: la nostra, with the feminine article. The full set is nostro, nostra, nostri, nostre.
'Questi sono i vostri posti' means 'These are your seats' (spoken to several people). Posti is masculine plural, so vostro becomes vostri with the article i: i vostri.
'Qual è la vostra opinione?' means 'What is your opinion?' (addressed to more than one person). Opinione is feminine singular, so vostro becomes vostra and takes la: la vostra.

'I bambini adorano il loro cane' means 'The children love their dog.' Loro (their) is invariable: it never changes for gender or number. Only the definite article moves: cane is masculine singular, so il loro.
examiner_note: loro invariable; the article still agrees (il, m.sg). must_not_include catches i loro (mistaking loro for a plural marker).

explanation: 'Le ragazze portano le loro borse' means 'The girls carry their bags.' Loro stays loro whatever the noun: it is invariable. The article does the agreeing: borse is feminine plural, so le loro.

prompt: Ho conosciuto ____ amico ieri. (a friend of mine)
explanation: 'Ho conosciuto un mio amico ieri' means 'I met a friend of mine yesterday.' Italian can place the indefinite article before the possessive: un mio amico, literally a my friend, for a friend of mine. The possessive still sits between article and noun.

'È una mia collega' means 'She is a colleague of mine.' The indefinite-article-plus-possessive pattern in the feminine: una mia collega. Collega is feminine here, so mia; the indefinite article is una.

'Marco ha perso i suoi occhiali' means 'Marco has lost his glasses.' Occhiali is masculine plural, so suo becomes suoi with the article i: i suoi. Suoi (masculine plural) contrasts with sue (feminine plural).

'La professoressa corregge i suoi compiti' means 'The teacher marks her homework.' Compiti is masculine plural, so sua becomes suoi, article i: i suoi, masculine, agreeing with the homework, not with the female teacher.
examiner_note: Owner-agreement trap in the plural: her -> i suoi because compiti is m.pl.

explanation: 'Giulia mette in valigia le sue scarpe' means 'Giulia packs her shoes.' Scarpe is feminine plural, so suo becomes sue with the article le: le sue.
'Le tue idee sono interessanti' means 'Your ideas are interesting.' Idee is feminine plural, so tuo becomes tue with the article le: le tue.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Mia madre' means 'my mother.' With a singular, unmodified family noun Italian drops the article: mia madre, not la mia madre. This family-noun exception covers close relatives in the singular.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Mio fratello' means 'my brother.' A singular, unmodified family noun takes no article: mio fratello, never il mio fratello. Fratello, sorella, madre, padre and the other close relatives all follow this.

'I miei fratelli' means 'my brothers.' The family-noun exception is singular only: as soon as the noun is plural, the article comes back, i miei fratelli, not miei fratelli. Contrast the singular mio fratello, which drops it.
'La mia mamma' means 'my mum.' The affectionate mamma (and babbo, dad) behaves unlike the neutral madre: it keeps the article, la mia mamma, where mia madre drops it. Warmth restores the article.

'Il loro padre' means 'their father.' Loro is the exception to the exception: with loro the article never drops, even for a singular family noun, il loro padre, not loro padre. Only mio, tuo, suo, nostro and vostro drop it with family.

prompt: Which is correct for 'my older sister'?

markpoints.label: modified family noun keeps the article

explanation: 'La mia sorella maggiore' means 'my older sister.' Once a family noun is modified, here by maggiore, the exception lapses and the article returns: la mia sorella maggiore. Bare mia sorella drops it, but mia sorella maggiore does not.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Sua sorella' means 'his sister' (or her sister, since suo agrees with sorella, not the owner). Sorella is a singular, unmodified family noun, so the article drops: sua sorella.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Tuo zio' means 'your uncle.' Zio is a close relative in the singular, so no article: tuo zio, not il tuo zio.

'Il mio ragazzo' means 'my boyfriend.' The drop applies only to family nouns: ragazzo (boyfriend) is not one, so it keeps the article, il mio ragazzo. Compare mio fratello, a relative, which drops it.
examiner_note: Same surface shape as mio fratello but the opposite answer; the conditioning factor is family-or-not.

explanation: 'I miei fratelli vivono all'estero' means 'My brothers live abroad.' In the plural the family-noun exception lapses, so the article returns: i miei fratelli. Producing miei fratelli without the article over-applies the exception.
examiner_note: Anchored must_not_include flags a dropped-article answer beginning 'miei'.

explanation: 'Le mie sorelle abitano a Torino' means 'My sisters live in Turin.' Plural family nouns take the article: le mie sorelle. The exception that drops it, mia sorella, holds only in the singular.
'I loro zii arrivano stasera' means 'Their uncles arrive tonight.' Two reasons the article stays: the noun is plural, and the possessive is loro, which always keeps the article. So i loro zii.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Nostra zia' means 'our aunt.' Zia is a singular close relative, so the article drops even with nostro: nostra zia, not la nostra zia.

markpoints.label: singular family noun drops the article

explanation: 'Mia moglie' means 'my wife.' Spouses count as family for the exception: mia moglie, mio marito, no article in the singular.

markpoints.label: modified family noun keeps the article

explanation: 'La mia cugina americana viene a trovarci' means 'My American cousin is coming to visit.' The modifier americana cancels the exception, so the article returns: la mia cugina americana. Bare mia cugina would drop it.

prompt: Il mio caffè è caldo; ____ com'è? (yours, informal)
explanation: 'Il mio caffè è caldo; il tuo com'è?' means 'My coffee is hot; how's yours?' Used on its own, the possessive pronoun still needs the article: il tuo (your one). It agrees with the understood noun caffè, masculine singular.

prompt: La mia risposta è giusta; anche ____ lo è. (his)
explanation: 'La mia risposta è giusta; anche la sua lo è' means 'My answer is right; his is too.' On its own the possessive keeps the article: la sua (his one), agreeing with the understood risposta, feminine singular.

prompt: I nostri posti sono qui; ____ sono là. (yours, plural)
explanation: 'I nostri posti sono qui; i vostri sono là' means 'Our seats are here; yours are there.' The standalone possessive takes the article and agrees with the understood posti, masculine plural: i vostri.

prompt: Le tue scarpe sono nuove; ____ sono vecchie. (mine)
explanation: 'Le tue scarpe sono nuove; le mie sono vecchie' means 'Your shoes are new; mine are old.' Standing in for scarpe (feminine plural), the possessive pronoun is le mie, article included.

prompt: Io ho portato il mio ombrello; Luca ha dimenticato ____. (his)
explanation: 'Io ho portato il mio ombrello; Luca ha dimenticato il suo' means 'I brought my umbrella; Luca forgot his.' As an object standing alone, the possessive still keeps the article: il suo, agreeing with ombrello, masculine singular.

prompt: Le vostre valigie sono leggere; ____ sono pesanti. (ours)
explanation: 'Le vostre valigie sono leggere; le nostre sono pesanti' means 'Your suitcases are light; ours are heavy.' The possessive pronoun le nostre replaces le nostre valigie, keeping article and feminine-plural agreement.

prompt: Which is correct for 'yours' in 'Il mio treno parte alle otto, ____ parte alle nove.' (informal)?
explanation: 'Il mio treno parte alle otto, il tuo parte alle nove' means 'My train leaves at eight, yours at nine.' A possessive pronoun standing on its own keeps the article: il tuo, not bare tuo.

prompt: Questa è la tua giacca? No, ____ è nera. (mine)
explanation: 'Questa è la tua giacca? No, la mia è nera' means 'Is this your jacket? No, mine is black.' The standalone possessive keeps the article and agrees with giacca, feminine singular: la mia.

'Paolo cerca le sue chiavi' means 'Paolo is looking for his keys.' Suo agrees with the thing owned: chiavi is feminine plural, so le sue, even though the owner Paolo is male. Sue is 'his' here, tracking the keys.
examiner_note: Discrimination bucket: suppressed pre-answer per criterion 15.

explanation: 'Sara cerca le sue chiavi' means 'Sara is looking for her keys.' Same form as for a male owner: le sue. Italian's suo/sua/suoi/sue does not encode the owner's gender, only the gender and number of the thing owned, here chiavi, feminine plural.
examiner_note: Pair with the 'his keys' item: identical answer le sue teaches that Italian does not distinguish his/her.

prompt: Which is correct for 'your glasses', speaking formally to Signor Bianchi: 'Signor Bianchi, ecco ____ occhiali.'?
explanation: 'Signor Bianchi, ecco i Suoi occhiali' means 'Mr Bianchi, here are your glasses.' Formal you (Lei) takes the third-person possessive Suo, often capitalised as a courtesy: i Suoi occhiali. Tuo would be informal, vostro plural.

'Lucia esce con il suo ragazzo' means 'Lucia is going out with her boyfriend.' Ragazzo is masculine, so il suo, and suo here means her, because suo agrees with the boyfriend, not with Lucia. English her tempts sua; resist it.

markpoints.label: suo agrees with the owned noun

explanation: 'Suo fratello' means 'her brother' (also his brother, since suo doesn't say which). Fratello is masculine, so suo, never sua, whatever the owner's gender. And as a singular family noun it drops the article: suo fratello.
